data.title: Moody's: Solid Q3 2024 Performance And Valuation Gap To SPGI Is Closing
data.description:
Investment action

I recommended a hold rating for Moody's Corporation (NYSE:MCO) when I wrote about it last month as I remained concerned about the relatively high valuation multiple vs. S&P Global Inc. (SPGI). However, I did believe that MCO fundamentals were much better, and I will turn bullish if the valuation comes down. While it has only been a month since my last update, I am writing an update to update my views in light of the earnings results released yesterday. I have gotten more bullish on MCO's fundamentals but am still reiterating my hold rating. MCO continues to trade at a premium to SPGI that I see as unsustainable as it implies strong earnings growth expectations (MCO stock fell ~4% yesterday despite the strong 3Q24 performance). The good news is that the valuation gap has gradually closed and an opportunity to invest in MCO may appear soon. Until then, investors should stay patient.

Review

MCO reported 3Q24 earnings yesterday. Total revenue came in 6% above consensus at $1.813 billion, representing 23.2% y/y growth. By segments, MIS (Moody's Investor Services) revenue grew 41.1% to $982 million, with growth seen across all sub-segments and primarily due to rated issuance that grew 51% in 3Q24. Solid MIS topline performance drove strong adj EBIT margin expansion from 53.2% to 59.6%. The MA (Moody's Analytics) segment saw revenue growth of 7.1% to $831 million and adj EBIT of $253 million (margin compressed by 330 bps y/y). Overall, EBITDA margins expanded 320 bps y/y to 47.8% due to the strong MIS segment performance, beating consensus expectations of 46.3%. As a result, MCO reported a very strong EPS beat, with adj EPS coming in at $3.21 vs. consensus of $2.87. This marked the third consecutive quarter of adjusted EPS beat. With the strong performance, MCO raised its FY24 guidance. Revenue growth is now expected to grow in the high-teens range from the prior low-teens range; EBITDA margins increased by 100 bps at the midpoint (from 46-47% to 47-48%); and EPS is now expected to be in the range of $11.90-12.10 from $11.00-11.40.

MCO fundamentals continue to get better, and it is reinforcing my positive view of the business fundamentals. On top of the solid ~36% y/y growth that the MIS segment saw last quarter (a two-year stack of 3Q24 performance) came in even stronger at a much larger magnitude, accelerating y/y growth by ~500 bps. What is notable about this growth is that on a two-year stack basis, growth accelerated by almost 18 points, a remarkable performance that indicates the underlying appetite for debt issuance. To give a sense of the strength behind this demand, note that management is now calling for MIS revenue growth of high-20% (from the prior high-teens guide), which reflects the strong 3Q24 performance and an improved outlook for 4Q. This guide is supported by strong-rated issuance growth, which is now expected to be in the mid-30s growth range vs. the prior 25 to 30%. I believe this guide is credible given management's track record of meeting its guidance (using the EPS guide as a benchmark, they met every year's guidance except for two years since FY13). Also, this guide is given with management having ~3 weeks of 4Q24 data, which should've given management a good sense of 4Q24 performance since they effectively have October's data and should already be engaging in talks for any upcoming issuance (i.e., pipeline).

An interesting takeaway from the earnings presentation was the solid growth potential over the mid-term. Firstly, speculative-grade debt has been the key driver of growth this quarter, and the increase over the years has resulted in an increasing number of refinancing needs over the next 3 to 4 years.

Secondly, with interest rates getting slashed across the globe (and is expected to continue doing so), this means a lower cost of financing for borrowers. Given the inverse relationship between rates and demand for debt, this is very positive for MCO. Thirdly, and this relates to the lower cost of debt, is the recovery in M&A. If you refer to the first chart above, it shows that the 10-year average M&A volume is around $5 trillion per year. However, because of the elevated rate environment, M&A volume fell to just $3.4 trillion last year and $3.1 trillion on a year-to-date basis. Assuming M&A volume recovers to the average of the next few years, this implies a massive surge in debt requirements. Thirdly, there are multiple new growth drivers that should result in more debt demand: private credit (both the MIS and MA segments are already booking private credit-related revenues); sustainable finance, emerging market, and cross-border domestic issuance; and digital finance (e.g., blockchain and tokenization). Lastly, the completion of the upcoming US presidential election should drive a recovery in the MA's segment Data and Information performance. This sub-segment ARR (annual recurring revenue) growth decelerated from 10% last quarter to 8% in 3Q24, partly due to the US election that has negatively impacted contract renewals with US government agencies as they reassess their spending plans.

Aside from the growth outlook, I believe MCO is also doing a solid job of rolling out more products and features to retain existing customers. For instance, MCO's investment in GenAI has enabled it to achieve higher customer satisfaction (those that use MCO's AI-enabled Research Assistant are demonstrating higher customer satisfaction scores). The recently launched automated credit memo and CRE early warning system should be well received too, which should improve retention rates and improve MCO's upsell opportunities.

Valuation

As I have mentioned repeatedly, the biggest hurdle to investing in MCO is the potential for valuation to be revised downwards (to where SPGI trades at). As you can see from the chart above, on a 1-year forward basis, MCO is trading at a premium to SPGI, but the good thing is the premium has gradually closed (from 20% when I first wrote, to 17% last month when I wrote about MCO, to the current 14%). On a 2-year forward basis, the valuation gap is now at 12%. I believe the market is gradually recognizing the valuation gap doesn't make sense and is correcting it. At the rate of this correction, I believe the opportunity to invest in MCO will appear soon. Note that despite this strong 3Q24 performance, MCO share price actually fell sharply by ~4%, indicating that expectations are still high.

Until then, I reiterate my hold rating and advise investors to be patient.

Risk

For readers that are feeling impatient, I like to note that MCO's growth outlook is not without risk. The credit market is still very much driven by the macro environment, and we are definitely not out of the woods yet as rates are still high, the job market is still not at its optimal condition, etc. For all we know, rates could stay higher for longer. The upcoming election is also a coin flip that could result in a resurge in inflation if tariffs are being implemented. Let's also not forget that we have multiple conflicts going on in the world today, which could easily result in a global modern conflict that is bad for the global economy.

Final thoughts

My recommendation is still a hold for MCO. On one hand, MCO's recent earnings report reinforces my view of its strong fundamentals, driven by robust growth in its core credit rating business. However, the stock still trades at a premium to SPGI that I find unsustainable. Fortunately, this gap is closing, which should provide patient investors with a much more favorable entry point.

data.title: WDI: Interest Rate Cuts Are A Positive Catalyst
data.description:
Overview

Western Asset Diversified Income Fund (NYSE:WDI) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide a high current income through a portfolio of different credit investments. Goldman recently published an article forecasting that the S&P 500 is likely to only return 3% over the next decade. Traditional investment vehicles like index funds typically rely on price appreciation to carry the majority of the returns, whereas credit investments deliver the returns through a high distribution. While we don't have a crystal ball to see whether or not future market movements play out this way, a scenario like this would make credit investments even more attractive.

WDI is appealing in this environment because of the current dividend yield of about 11.6%. Looking at WDI, we can see that the price has increased by a solid 9.18%. Whether a return is captured through capital appreciation or a high distribution yield, the math comes out the same. The difference is that a return captured through collected distributions feels a bit more real in comparison to unrealized gains. Additionally, this sort of dividend yield makes WDI an attractive choice for income focused investors that may be looking for a source of supplemental income to fund lifestyle expenses.

Additionally, I believe that the current economic environment makes this sort of credit investing very attractive. While rates remain close to decade highs, the portfolio of investments may produce higher amounts of net investment income that can support the distribution with ease. What makes WDI unique is that the fund has a public inception dating back to only 2021, which means that the fund hasn't had the opportunity to really operate in a more typical interest rate environment, meaning that entry here could be highly attractive. Let's first start by assessing the fund's strategy and underlying asset exposure.

Fund Strategy & Performance

In order to support the higher distribution yield, WDI has a portfolio with total assets sitting at $1.18B. According to the fund overview, high-yield credit investments account for the largest weight of assets at 38.73%. This is followed by exposure to CLOs (collateralized loan obligations) and bank loans, accounting for 12.42% and 11.32% respectively. In order to generate the higher yield to support the high distribution, these credit investments typically have lower credit ratings and include a higher level of vulnerability. These generally include varying types of corporate loans to companies that have weaker balance sheets.

Additionally, these investments are typically issued on a floating rate basis. As a result, we can see higher levels of net investment income be generated and result in a stronger distribution coverage. Looking at the most recent annual report from 2023, we can see that the level of net investment income gradually increased alongside the increase of interest rates. We can see that net investment income landed at $1.73 per share over 2023 and net realized gains amounted to $0.68 per share. Thankfully, the higher level of net investment income received fully supported the distribution for the year.

Although the 2024 report has not been released yet, we can assume that net realized gains and net investment income were also strong for this year, since the price has steadily increased on a YTD basis. The more excess realized gains there are, the greater NAV growth that can be experienced. We can see that the NAV has deteriorated from $19.65 per share during 2021, down to $15.54 per share at the end of 2023. I expect this to improve since future interest rate cuts can provide some relief and contribute to growth of underlying borrowers.

Risk Profile

As mentioned, the credit quality ratings of the underlying portfolio of borrowers is typically of lesser quality. The more risk that a borrower has, the higher the cash flow that can be collected from them. WDI's portfolio is spread across 345 different holdings, and we can see that the majority of the holdings sit at a credit rating of BB, accounting for nearly 30%. For reference, anything with a rating of BB+ or lower is considered to be below investment grade, or commonly referred to as 'junk'. Ratings of a B and CCC account for an allocation of 27.03% and 11.48% respectively.

The downside here is that these types of borrowers are more prone to defaults because they are less resilient to any headwinds. Elevated interest rates are a common headwind that these businesses face since they are very reliant on debt financing to expand operations. As the interest rates sit near their decade high, borrowers are seeing increased debt maintenance costs in the form of higher interest payments. Naturally, this leads to an increase in defaults, but this can be mitigated by strong underwriting criteria from management.

Data compiled by S&P Global supports this notion that defaults tend to increase alongside rising rates and during times of market retractions. We can see that speculative grade corporate borrowers saw a rise in defaults during moments like 2009's great financial crisis, and 2020's pandemic induced market drop. The data is only updated through the end of 2023, but we can see that the annual global rate of defaults is approaching 4%. CLOs are also a part of WDI's portfolio, and they have historically held up much better compared to these corporate loans.

Although I believe interest rates will continue to be reduced over the next year, there's always a chance that we remain in a higher interest rate environment. This would create an ongoing risk of higher defaults for WDI, resulting in suppressed price movements.

Valuation & Outlook

Since WDI operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. Looking at the charts below, we can see that the discount to NAV reached as high as 16% back in 2022. However, the price currently trades at a very modest discount to NAV of only 1.16%. For reference, the price of WDI traded at an average discount to NAV of 9.26% over the last three years. Despite the discount to NAV no longer being as larger as it once was, I believe that WDI offers great value here because of my anticipation of future interest rate cuts.

Since the majority of the underlying assets in WDI's portfolio are debt investments that operate on a floating rate basis, we can see how the price has an inverse relationship to the federal funds rate. Shortly after the fund launched, the Fed started to aggressively hike interest rates throughout 2022 and 2023 to a decade high. Higher interest rates directly correlate to a higher amount of net investment income, but can suppress valuations since the cost of debt increases and negatively impacts operating margins. A higher rate may also suppress valuations since the rate of defaults naturally start to increase as a result of the increased cost of debt maintenance.

Therefore, I believe that we can expect the price of WDI to gradually increase as interest rates are cut. Although rate cuts will negatively impact earnings potential of the underlying floating rate investments, it simultaneously creates an environment that is more attractive for borrowers. An environment of lower rates helps to enable valuation growth since this borrowed capital can be allocated towards growth initiatives at cheaper rates.

The Fed recently implemented its first set of interest rate cuts in mid-September of 50 basis points. Economic data is leaning towards the likelihood of additional interest rate cuts happening. For instance, the unemployment rate has steadily increased over the last year and now sits at 4.1%. As more households have trouble in the labor market, we can expect unfavorable shifts in consumer spending.

In addition, inflation levels are still above the Fed's targeted 2% rate as of the latest September report. Lastly, we have the US Presidential elections still upcoming in November and this may create a market environment of higher volatility and uncertainty. The combination of these factors may incentivize the Fed to implement additional interest rate cuts over the course of the next year as a way to provide stimulus.

Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly dividend of $0.14850 per share, the current dividend yield sits at 11.6%. The combination of a double-digit yield and monthly pay frequency makes WDI an attractive option for investors looking to capture a consistent stream of supplemental income. So far, the distribution has consistently trended upward and rewarded investors with a steadily increasing dividend yield. As mentioned, the net investment income for 2023 came in at $1.73 per share and net realized gains came in at $0.68 per share, fully covering the annual dividend. Assuming a similar performance for 2024, I expect earnings to continue covering the distribution.

Since the yield is in the double digits, I was curious as to how well supported the distribution is. Thankfully, the latest distribution details reveal that it has been funded entirely by net investment income. This helps add confidence that a meaningful level of NAV growth is captured while also fully funding the distribution. Thankfully, there is no use of return of capital here to add to any NAV erosion as well.

However, it's worth noting that the distributions received from WDI are classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividends have less favorable tax consequences compared to the qualified dividends you'd receive from traditional dividend stocks. Therefore, investors may want to consider utilizing WDI in a tax advantaged account to offset the tax burden that may come from the distributions.

Takeaway

As a result, I rate WDI as a buy because of its diverse portfolio that may benefit from future interest rate cuts. Not only would interest rate cuts provide current borrowers some relief, but it would simultaneously create a more favorable environment for new borrowers seeking capital. Lower interest rates would provide an environment that helps support increased valuations over time as well. The combination of net investment income and net realized gains continue to support the distribution of 11.6% so far and as performance improves, there is a probability of continued dividend increases.

data.title: BIL: With Interest Rates Declining, Consider Pairing Back Cash And T-Bills
data.description:
T-bills have been one of the best-performing income asset classes these past few years, as Federal Reserve cuts led to significantly higher interest rates on these securities and lower bond prices on most bonds. With the Fed finally cutting rates, t-bill yields will decline, and outperformance will likely cease. Although t-bills and other cash instruments will always have a place in an investor's portfolio, these developments do decrease their attractiveness compared to most bonds. As such, investors should consider reducing or replacing their cash investments, especially those overweight on cash.

I'll be focusing on the SPDR® Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (NYSEARCA:BIL) for the remainder of this article, but everything here should apply to other t-bill ETFs, and to the asset class itself.

BIL - Quick Overview and Investment Thesis

BIL invests in t-bills, securities backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. Federal Government, the largest, most credit-worthy institution in the world. T-bills have effectively zero credit risk, seeing zero losses during downturns and recessions, compared to significant losses for riskier high-yield bonds and equities. BIL was not impacted by the pandemic, compared to double-digit drawdowns for these asset classes.

Federal Reserve hikes have led to sharply higher interest rates across bonds and fixed-income securities. Short-term and variable rate investments have seen particularly big impacts, including t-bills. BIL itself yields more than treasuries and most investment-grade bonds, less than high-yield bonds.

BIL has outperformed since early 2022, as hikes led to lower prices for most bonds. T-bills were not impacted, as these are ultra-short-term securities.

BIL's above-average dividend yield also played a role in the above, although a smaller one. As an example, BIL has outperformed most bonds YTD even though the Fed has cut rates, partly due to its higher yield (sentiment and expectations also played a role).

T-bills are a comparatively high-yield, low-risk investment opportunity right now, but that won't be the case for long. Federal Reserve cuts will lead to lower t-bill rates, with the Fed already cutting rates 0.50%. A 0.50% decrease in t-bill rates is a given, with more cuts / decreases almost certain. The exact magnitude is, of course, not certain, but the trend definitely is.

Current Federal Reserve guidance implies the following t-bill rates:

As can be seen above, the Fed is guiding for another 0.5% in cuts this year, another 1.0% next year, and 0.5% thereafter. Assuming guidance holds true, t-bill rates should decline below those of investment-grade bonds in early 2025, and 10-year treasuries in mid-2025.

In my opinion, and considering recent Fed cuts, guidance, and current market conditions, t-bill yields will almost certainly decline below those of most bonds in the coming years. The specifics are subject to change, and I could imagine yields above average for a year or two, but not much longer. Conditions are such for that to happen, and it would take a significant Fed pivot for this to change.

Due to the above, t-bills should underperform most bonds in the medium and long term moving forward. BIL itself has underperformed since its inception, as expected. T-bills outperforming is uncommon, and the situation should normalize in the coming months.

Many investors chose to overweight cash, t-bills, and similar investments in prior years, as economic conditions seemed (and were) favorable to these. Said decision was proven right, with t-bills and the like outperforming these past few years. Economic conditions are turning less favorable, so investors should consider selling or pairing back their cash or t-bill holdings. Some cash always makes sense, but being overweight cash does not, in my opinion at least.

With the above in mind, let's take a look at some possible replacements for cash, t-bills, and BIL.

BIL - Replacements

The Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA) could be a fantastic replacement for BIL. JAAA focuses on AAA-rated CLOs, with negligible credit and rate risk, and significantly below-average volatility. JAAA almost always trades with a 1.0% - 1.5% spread to BIL, right now sporting a 6.4% dividend yield, 1.2% higher than BIL.

Returns have been much higher too, if a bit more volatile (still much less than average though).

JAAA's 1.2% spread to t-bills should remain the same magnitude as the Fed cuts rates. The importance of said spread should increase though and might make all the difference if rates plummet. Cuts of 5.0% would leave t-bills yielding effectively zero, but JAAA would maintain a 1.2% yield, providing some income to shareholders. As an example, JAAA delivered 2.2% from mid-2020 to early 2022, compared to negative 0.1% for BIL. JAAA delivered some income even though rates were at zero, BIL delivered less than nothing.

JAAA's higher dividend yield and broadly similar characteristics make it a suitable replacement for BIL.

Notwithstanding the above, JAAA suffers from many of the same issues as BIL, as the funds are very similar (effectively zero credit and rate risk, directly exposed to Fed rates). Going for funds with a little bit more credit risk and duration seems better, as these have higher, more sustainable yields. I've covered four particularly strong replacement ETFs these past few months, the key table is as follows:

Of the ETFs above, the JPMorgan Income ETF (JPIE) is closest to BIL, with a duration of 3.0 years, and with the fund focusing on investment-grade / AAA-rated securities.

JPIE has underperformed BIL since its inception:

But outperformed YTD:

In my opinion, JPIE will continue to outperform relative to t-bills moving forward, as the interest rates on the latter decrease due to Fed cuts.

JPIE is closest to BIL, but the Angel Oak Income ETF (CARY) is my top choice. It has broadly similar characteristics to JPIE, but with a slightly higher 6.6% yield, but greater risk and volatility. Returns have been marginally higher since inception, comfortably so YTD.

Besides JPIE and CARY, the Capital Group U.S. Multi-Sector Income ETF (CGMS) and the iShares Flexible Income Active ETF (BINC) are also solid choices.

Do bear in mind that there are lots of even higher-yielding funds than the four above, but these are all comparatively safe, low-volatility choices, and more suitable replacements for BIL than most.

Conclusion

Federal Reserve cuts will decrease t-bill rates, and lead to declining dividends for BIL. Investors should consider selling or reducing their exposure to this and similar assets. ETFs focusing on shorter-term, high-quality securities seem like particularly suitable alternatives. These include JPIE, CARY, BINC, and CGMS.

data.title: Artisan Mid Cap Fund Q3 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investing Environment

Investors went into Q3 waiting for an all-clear sign on inflation that would let the Fed finally cut interest rates. Economic data during the period pointed in that direction, with soft readings on employment and inflation.

Consumer price index (CPI) data generally trended lower over the quarter, with the most recent August core CPI reading showing a 3.2% year-over-year increase, the lowest since 2021. Meanwhile, non-farm payrolls rose by just 114,000 jobs in July, well below the consensus expectation of 175,000, and the unemployment rate rose to 4.3%. The jobs report sparked fears that the Fed may have waited too long to cut interest rates and risked damaging the economy. Fed Chair Jerome Powell used his Jackson Hole speech to signal an interest rate cut in September, and the Federal Open Market Committee followed through with a 50bps cut.

Elsewhere, the Bank of England and the European Central Bank also cut rates by 25bps in the quarter. In contrast, the Bank of Japan surprised the market by raising rates, resulting in a stronger yen and triggering an unwinding of a popular US dollar-Japanese yen carry trade, which caused a short-lived spike in volatility.

Markets experienced a style and sector performance rotation in Q3. Looking at the Russell 1000® Index, performance in the first half of the year was dominated by a narrow set of US mega-cap winners (the Magnificent Seven). This narrow leadership meant growth outperformed value, large caps outperformed small caps, and sector performance was led by information technology and communication services. Results in Q3 were the opposite: value outperformed growth, small caps outperformed large caps, and previously shunned sectors, such as utilities and real estate, gained renewed favor with investors.

Although US markets were robust, non-US markets also saw gains, most notably emerging markets. Late in the quarter, the People's Bank of China announced a major stimulus package that led to one of the sharpest and most concentrated rallies in Chinese market history, as investors hoped the measures would reinvigorate the world's second-largest economy.

Performance Discussion

Our portfolio underperformed the Russell Midcap® Growth Index in Q3. Underperformance was led by negative security selection within health care, information technology and industrials.

Within health care, several holdings, including West Pharmaceutical Services (WST), DexCom (DXCM), iRhythm (IRTC) and Edwards Lifesciences (EW), experienced idiosyncratic challenges. However, the drag on performance from those holdings was partially offset by our biotechnology holdings, as Argenx (ARGX) and Exact Sciences (EXAS) had strong quarters. Challenges within information technology were mostly due to cloud software investments, such as Atlassian (TEAM), Synopsys (SNPS) and HubSpot (HUBS), as they continued to deal with softer small and medium-sized business spending due to macroeconomic headwinds, along with shifting enterprise IT spending toward artificial intelligence projects at the expense of traditional cloud software offerings. Lastly, two Crop SM holdings in industrials, SAIA and Trex, reported fundamental results that were weaker than investor expectations.

From an allocation perspective, the portfolio’s underweight positioning in utilities and overweight in information technology and health care detracted from relative results. This was partially offset, however, by our underweight to the underperforming energy and consumer staples sectors.

Among our top detractors were Dexcom, iRhythm and Celsius. Dexcom is the leader in continuous glucose-monitoring (CGM) systems. With data increasingly supporting the clinical and economic case for CGMs, we believe Dexcom is well positioned to further penetrate the Type 1 diabetes market and to drive adoption in the much larger Type 2 diabetes market. Unfortunately, financial results showed meaningful growth deceleration, and shares responded accordingly. The company pointed to several causes for the surprising slowdown, most of which were execution related (e.g., sales force changes and distribution channel mismanagement) in the context of healthy industry trends. While we continue to believe in the case for CGMs and Dexcom’s technology, the lack of execution is concerning. We believe these operational mistakes will take time to fix, and we reduced our position.

IRhythm develops and markets the Zio, a small, wearable patch that monitors suspected heartbeat arrhythmias. We believe the company’s superior technology will be widely adopted as physicians and payors look to detect and treat atrial fibrillation before patients experience strokes. And we expect to see strong execution toward these goals by the relatively new management team. Unfortunately, several regulatory issues have emerged over the last year, with roots going back to the company’s earlier years. We believe these are all fixable (with progress to be seen soon), but these developments have clearly weighed on the stock’s performance. We remain patient in a Garden SM position.

Celsius is an energy drink company viewed as providing a healthier option than its large competitors. Our thesis was that Celsius' product portfolio appeals to a broad demographic, creating new consumers and generating more frequent usage occasions for the category.

Unfortunately, in recent months we have witnessed a sudden slowdown in both the company’s market share gains and growth in the broader energy drink category. We had expected its progress and overall market expansion to continue in 2024. With evidence to the contrary, we significantly trimmed our position.

Among our top Q3 contributors were Argenx, Spotify and Exact Sciences. Shares of Argenx have rallied this year due to multiple positive developments. The company recently received FDA approval to promote VYVGART® for chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (CIDP). We expect this to be the drug’s second blockbuster indication since it is the first meaningful advance in CIDP treatment in decades. Meanwhile, VYVGART®’s first approved indication in myasthenia gravis continues to shine. With sales beating analyst expectations, we believe the size of the myasthenia gravis opportunity will continue to yield upside longer term. Lastly, the company is investing in clinical trials studying VYVGART® in numerous other rare autoimmune disorders and is making good progress in advancing its second breakthrough medicine.

Spotify is a leading global audio streaming franchise with over 600 million monthly active users. We believe its position in the supply chain is solid given a secular trend of fragmentation in the music industry as well as internal product and pricing initiatives. Shares rallied after the company reported strong earnings results, including 20% revenue growth. Importantly, the company’s profit margin is expanding nicely, and we believe it can rise further due to likely price increases, potentially better terms with labels and further cost discipline.

Exact Sciences is a leading provider of diagnostic testing and a maker of the noninvasive colorectal cancer screening test Cologuard®. Shares fell in the first half of the year due to investor concerns around competing blood-based tests. However, we remained invested because blood tests, while potentially more convenient, have historically been unable to match the accuracy of stool testing (especially when it comes to detecting early cancers), and we believed its valuation failed to capture Cologuard’s significant long-term growth and margin expansion opportunity. Our patience was rewarded when the company reported accelerating Cologuard revenue growth (from 7% to 15%). Growth is also expected to improve further in the second half of the year as Exact Sciences partners with hospital networks and payors to meet colon cancer screening targets before year-end. Furthermore, the company disclosed early data for its own blood-based colon cancer screening test. While not as accurate as Cologuard, this test may prove to be more predictive and cheaper than competing blood tests.

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter, we initiated new Garden SM positions in CBRE Group, Tetra Tech and Coherent. CBRE Group is the largest global commercial real estate broker with leading positions in its three business units: market advisory (property leasing, capital markets and mortgage servicing), global workforce solutions (facilities management and project management) and real estate investments. We believe the commercial real estate market may be bottoming as the rising interest rate headwind begins to ease. As the industry conditions begin to thaw, the company should see increased brokerage volumes and asset management returns. In addition, 51% of revenues are from its Workforce Solutions business, where CBRE is taking advantage of a secular trend toward facilities management outsourcing by expanding its capabilities to serve specialized facilities such as data centers and laboratories.

Tetra Tech is a leader in the water, renewable energy and sustainable infrastructure end markets. The company provides exposure to an ongoing sustainability trend and funding renaissance around water. For example, the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) recently finalized the National Primary Drinking Water Regulation, which states that public water systems must monitor and limit per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in drinking water. Coupled with an experienced management team, established client relationships and key capabilities, we believe Tetra Tech should be a long-term beneficiary of the transformational federal funding and secular tailwinds of global investment into water infrastructure.

Coherent is a global leader in engineered materials, optoelectronic components and lasers. The company is experiencing strong AI- related demand for its data center optical networking products, which serves a key industry bottleneck as customers try to optimize the efficiency of their AI investments. We believe this AI exposure and an upturn in its more cyclical segments should lead to meaningful multiyear revenue growth. Meanwhile, we believe its new CEO, whom we know well from our campaign in Lattice Semiconductor, will drive meaningful change in the business, including divesting slow-growth business segments and driving much-needed operational improvements to unlock margin expansion opportunities.

Notable adds in the quarter included Samsara (IOT), Illumina (ILMN) and Onto Innovation (ONTO). Samsara is a software platform for asset-intensive industries that provides data and insights to enable asset tracking, performance measurement and safety monitoring. We believe the company’s attractive value proposition for customers makes it a durable top-line compounder that will become highly free cash flow generative over time. Recent quarterly results displayed the company's rapid growth, and we believe it remains in the early stages of its profit cycle based on the size of its addressable markets. We added to the position.

Illumina is a leading provider of next-generation sequencing instruments for genetic testing. As costs have fallen, genome sequencing has become more mainstream, expanding within academic research and beyond into high-value clinical diagnostic testing applications. The company has gone through a difficult few years after an unwise acquisition of Grail (a company that develops blood tests designed to detect cancers), a difficult transition to a new sequencing system and significant margin contraction. However, new management has focused on controlling costs, has spun out Grail and, in our view, is putting the company back on a path of sustainable growth. With a stock price that we do not believe reflects Illumina’s value as a unique life science tools franchise, we continue to build our Garden SM position.

Onto Innovation provides process control solutions and inspection systems needed for advanced semiconductor packaging inspection and optical metrology. Wafer-level packaging inspection has been a small yet rapidly growing segment within process control tied to increasing chipset sales from AI, edge computing and wearable technology advancements. Optical metrology growth has been driven by a transition to 3D chip architecture, which requires greater numbers of sensitive layers to be measured and tracked. This growth has been further supported by gross margin and operating margin expansion, as increasing complexity drives pricing power. Shares declined in the quarter, and we used it as an opportunity to build our position.

We ended our investment campaigns in Edwards Lifesciences, Jabil and Trex during the quarter. Edwards Lifesciences is the leader in minimally invasive structural heart disease therapies. While we remain optimistic about the company’s new product pipeline of devices to treat mitral and tricuspid valve disorders, we were disappointed to see a meaningful slowdown in the company’s core transcatheter aortic valve replacement franchise (TAVR). At 64% of revenues, TAVR’s slow growth—which seems to be a function of capacity constraints in its customer base—makes it difficult for Edwards to meet our growth expectations, and we decided to exit our position.

Jabil provides outsourced manufacturing services to diverse end markets and customers. Our thesis was that the company was moving away from the more cyclical consumer electronics markets toward secular growth areas, such as electric vehicles and medical devices, which would lead to faster growth and higher margins. Unfortunately, while it is a diversified contract manufacturer, several of its meaningful markets have come under pressure this year, including residential solar, semiconductor capital equipment and health care devices. While these headwinds will likely prove temporary, we have also been disappointed by a high level of management turnover in recent quarters. Given these issues, we decided to exit the position in favor of higher conviction investments in technology.

Trex manufactures non-wood decking products. Our view has been that Trex would be able to withstand the cyclical slowdown in housing-related spending as it has been gaining market share thanks to greater consumer awareness of composite decking’s low-to-no maintenance features and new lower priced options to broaden its potential customer base. Unfortunately, recent earnings results called this thesis into question as the company has been dealing with elevated inventory levels and disappointing sales in its lower priced options. Given these challenges, we sold our position.

Along with Dexcom and Celsius, a notable trim in the quarter was HubSpot. HubSpot is a leading cloud-based customer relationship management software provider for small-to-medium businesses. The stock was a top performer in 2023 as it meaningfully improved its profitability after several years of heavy investment. However, as we mentioned earlier in this letter, the environment for cloud software providers has been challenging in 2024 as macroeconomic pressures have impacted customer spending. Our long-term conviction remains intact, but we reduced the position due to near-term uncertainty.

Meanwhile, we are encouraged by the company’s efforts to leverage AI advances to help internally (e.g., more efficient software development) and externally (e.g., new agent-based apps to help customers extract more value out of its products).

Stewardship Update

In 2024, our team has been spending more time learning about key water sustainability issues—from not only the business risks presented by water challenges but also the opportunities for companies that help to address these challenges. One specific area we have been researching is water contamination from PFAS (per- and poly-fluoroalkyl substances) in drinking water. These chemicals were widely used in various industrial applications, including nonstick cookware, fire-fighting foams and stain repellents. The problem is that these chemicals do not break down easily and can contaminate water sources for many years, and studies have linked PFAS exposure to various health issues.

To combat this, governments are in the very early stages of implementing stricter regulations (and providing funding) to limit the use and release of PFAS and advocating for advanced water treatment technologies to remove PFAS from drinking water. As awareness and regulations regarding the health risks associated with PFAS contamination increase, the need for effective solutions to remove these chemicals from drinking water also grows. For example, recent news publications have pointed to the contamination of farmland due to the utilization of “sludge fertilizer” sourced from municipal sewage systems. When a fertilizer containing PFAS is applied to farmland, the chemicals get into the soil and water. This creates a market opportunity for companies that provide filtration systems, water treatment services, testing and monitoring equipment, and technical consulting services, such as Tetra Tech and Xylem.

Perspective

After months of speculation, the market received its much hoped-for first interest rate cut, justifying the year-to-date fall in the two-year US Treasury yield of -61bps (from 4.25% to 3.64%). It is still up for debate how far these cuts will go given the wide range of future possibilities, including an economic hard landing that brings a recession and aggressive Fed rate cutting, a soft landing with modest growth and controlled inflation, and a "no landing" where the Fed is unable to move much further due to stubborn inflationary pressures. We are not making a prediction but will monitor these trends closely.

In general, even if the rate-cutting cycle proves modest, we believe it is healthy for businesses exposed to construction and real estate. For example, Equifax, CBRE Group (discussed earlier) and NVR have been pressured in recent years by high mortgage rates and challenging commercial real estate fundamentals, but we believe those dynamics are starting to ease, and we could be entering an attractive new cycle.

Of course, the looming US election will likely have at least some impact on markets, especially in areas such as renewable energy, antitrust regulation and corporate tax rates. While we believe certain portfolio holdings would be advantaged or disadvantaged by certain election outcomes, our experience has taught us not to position the portfolio for a specific result.

At a sector level, we think the most important market debate going forward is the strength of spending on AI. We will be closely watching to see if the large AI investments made by companies translate to productivity gains or competitive advantages. If they are, AI capital expenditures will likely continue to be strong. However, future spending could be at risk if these investments do not yield real results. We are focused on AI enablers that we believe remain well positioned to benefit from reasonable (not even the best case) industry investment levels because they are gaining market share based on superior technologies (e.g., higher speed and/or lower power), such as Arista Networks, Monolithic Power Systems, Onto Innovation, Synopsys and Coherent. Within industrials, electrification enablers like Quanta Services and Hubbell are well placed to benefit from not only AI data center construction but also the national need for more extensive and reliable electrical grids to support the energy transition. While we expect some volatility in AI-exposed stocks given relatively lofty expectations, we are being disciplined on valuations and are optimistic these can be attractive investments over time.

data.title: Health and Happiness: The Good And The Bad
data.description:
My rating for Health and Happiness (H&H) International Holdings Limited (OTCPK:BTSDF) is a Hold. The good thing regarding H&H is the positive growth outlook for its supplements business. The bad thing about BTSDF is its infant milk formula business' unfavorable prospects. The stock is now trading at a Price-to-Earnings Growth or PEG multiple of about 1x, and this means that the company's expected earnings growth rate is factored into its earnings multiple. As such, I maintain a Hold rating for H&H.

H&H describes itself as a "health and nutrition company for the whole family" on the company's website. BTSDF generated 66% and 27% of its 1H 2024 top line from nutritional supplements and infant milk formulas, respectively. The Mainland Chinese market contributed over two-thirds or 68% of H&H's 2024 interim revenue. These figures are taken from the company's 2024 interim report. My focus is on H&H's nutritional supplements and infant milk formula businesses in China for the current article, taking into consideration the company's revenue mix.

BTSDF's 2020 interim results were the subject of my earlier September 29, 2020 write-up.

Investors should be aware that they can deal in H&H's shares on the Over-The-Counter market and the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong. The trading liquidity for the company's OTC shares is poor. But readers can trade in H&H's relatively more liquid Hong Kong-listed shares with US brokerages like Interactive Brokers or Hong Kong securities firms such as Boom Securities. As a reference, the three-month average daily trading value for the company's Hong Kong-listed shares was roughly $500,000 based on S&P Capital IQ data.

Supplements Business Is A Leading Player In A Growing Market

H&H reports its full financial results on a semi-annual basis as a Hong Kong-listed company. The company's recent 1H 2024 top line performance was mixed with the strength of the supplements business offsetting the weakness of the infant milk formula business. On a constant currency basis, BTSDF's VHMS (Vitamins, Herbs, Minerals & Supplements) product sales grew by +13.0% YoY in the 2024 interim period, while its infant milk formula product revenue decreased by -18.8% YoY during the same time frame.

I will write about H&H's supplements and infant milk formula businesses in this section and the next section, respectively.

There are good reasons to believe that the positive revenue growth momentum for BTSDF's supplements business can be sustained in the foreseeable future. The supplements market in China has significant growth potential, and H&H is a market leader in this space.

The value of "China's health supplements market" has doubled from RMB 161.4 billion in 2017 to RMB328.2 billion in 2023, according to a July 16, 2024, article posted on China Briefing, a China-focused media publication established by consulting firm Dezan Shira & Associates.

It is realistic to think that the Chinese supplements industry has a long growth runway.

As per Chinese consumer research firm CBNData's January 2024 write-up (translated using Google), the average amount of money that a consumer spends on supplements in China on a yearly basis is less than a fifth of that for other countries like Germany and the US.

Separately, Chinese online retail giant JD.com, Inc. (JD) disclosed on its blog that "sales of probiotics and health tonics increased more than tenfold and threefold, respectively" on the company's platform at this year's China 618 e-commerce festival. China's per-capita spending on supplements is expected to increase going forward, as consumers in the country become more health-conscious as evidenced by the recent health product sales data shared by JD.

H&H's supplements business should be able to capitalize on the long-term growth of China's supplements industry with its leading market position.

In its investor presentation slides, BTSDF highlighted that its Swisse brand is the second-largest player in China's overall VHMS sector. The company's Swisse brand is ranked first in the online segment of the Chinese VHMS segment and certain supplement categories like "beauty, women's health, men's health, detox, heart health, joint health" in China as per its investor presentation disclosures. In the July 16, 2024, China Briefing article referred to above, it was indicated that the Swiss brand boasted a 29% market share in the "vitamins and minerals" segment of China's supplements market.

Safety is definitely a key consideration for consumers selecting supplement brands, and trusted brands are often the leading brands that are known to most people. This means that top brands like Swisse will most likely retain their market leadership in the absence of any negative events affecting their brand equity and reputation.

In summary, the Chinese supplements market has good growth prospects, and BTSDF's Swisse brand is one of the top players in the China market. This bodes well for H&H's VHMS sales outlook.

But Infant Milk Formula Business Faces Headwinds

BTSDF's infant milk formula business segment operating under the Biostime brand has to deal with challenges like a decreasing birth rate and stiff competition in China.

A declining birth rate for the Chinese market translates into a smaller addressable market for H&H's infant milk formula products.

China's birth rate measured in "births per 1,000 inhabitants" was 6.39 last year according to data sourced from Statista. As a comparison, the country's annual birth rate was consistently above 12 between 2000 and 2017. Reuters recently published an article on October 18, 2024, mentioning that "China's National Health Commission" has initiated a survey to "understand factors influencing" the "fear surrounding having children." The low birth rate in China is a serious issue, and it isn't entirely certain what has deterred many Chinese from having more babies which has justified a new survey from the Chinese government. This implies that China's birth rate could stay low for the foreseeable future.

The Chinese dairy sector has taken a hit from a declining number of new births in China. A September 24, 2024, Newsweek report noted that there is "an oversupply of milk amid a flagging birth rate" and highlighted the "significantly reduced demand for infant formula" in the country. A good proxy for the health of China's infant milk formula industry is China Feihe Limited's (OTCPK:CFEIY) (OTCPK:CHFLF) [6186:HK] top line performance. China Feihe is the biggest infant milk formula company in China as per Frost & Sullivan research as indicated on its website. China Feihe's revenue in local currency or RMB terms contracted by -6.4% and -8.3% for FY 2022 and FY 2023, respectively as per S&P Capital IQ data.

On the other hand, H&H has ceded ground to its rivals in a shrinking Chinese infant milk formula market.

It is worth noting that China Feihe's top line performance improved in 1H 2024 with a +3.7% YoY revenue growth. In contrast, H&H's infant milk formula sales for the China market decreased by -19.0% YoY during the same time period. In the company's investor presentation slides, H&H cited "increased competitive intensity" as a headwind for its infant milk formula business, and it revealed that its market share in the overall China infant milk formula decreased by -0.6 percentage points YoY to 4.7% in 1H 2024.

H&H is ranked seventh with a 4.7% market share in terms of total infant milk formula sales for the Chinese market. But BTSDF is only the 11th largest player with regard to infant milk formula sales for the e-commerce sales channel in the country boasting a relatively lower 3.1% market share. It is reasonable to think that H&H has a comparatively weaker competitive position in the e-commerce sales channel, which could have hurt its overall infant milk formula sales in China.

In a nutshell, H&H is facing substantial headwinds like tougher competition and a decreasing number of childbirths in China.

Final Thoughts

H&H's overall top line decreased by -4.1% YoY in 1H 2024, which was far worse than the company's 1H 2023 revenue expansion of +17.2% YoY. On a full-year basis, BTSDF's revenue growth is projected to slow significantly from +9.0% last year to +0.3% this year as per S&P Capital IQ data. While the long-term growth outlook for H&H's supplements business is favorable, the company's short-term top line performance has been negatively impacted by the weakness of its infant milk formula business. It is fair to say there are both good and bad businesses within the company.

BTSDF's mixed prospects relating to both its supplements and infant milk formula businesses are appropriately reflected in the stock's valuations. H&H is currently trading at a PEG (Price-to-Earnings Growth) or PEG multiple of 1.1 times, which suggests that the stock is fairly valued. As per S&P Capital IQ data, the company's consensus FY 2023-2026 normalized EPS CAGR is an unexciting +8%, while the stock trades at a modest 9 times consensus next twelve months' normalized P/E.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FXI: I Expect The Chinese Euphoria To Pass (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Main Thesis/Background

The purpose of this article is to discuss the iShares China Large-Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:FXI) as an investment option at its current market price. This is a reasonable option for domestic investors who want exposure to China as the fund's objective is to "track the investment results of an index composed of large-capitalization Chinese equities that trade on the Hong Kong Exchange".

It has been a while since I covered FXI - or Chinese equities more broadly - but readers would remember I have been a bull in the past. In fact, since my last positive review, FXI has indeed posted a positive return. However, this pales in comparison to what US stock markets have delivered in the interim:

Given this reality, I thought now was a decent time to take another look at FXI. After all, since the Chinese government announced stimulus efforts to revive their economy, FXI has been on a tear:

This momentum has certainly been making plenty of noise, but the fact is it has stalled a bit in the immediate term. For me, I think the gains in Chinese equities, and FXI by extension, have come in a bit too far, too fast. This leads me to a more cautious stance going forward, which I will outline below. The net result is I am shifting from a "buy" to a "hold" rating, as I feel it would be prudent to wait for a better opportunity in the future.

It Seems Like Everyone Has Rushed In

The very first point I want to touch on is that I think this play has gotten overblown on a broad scale. What I mean by this is that it seems like everyone and their mother have been rushing into Chinese equities. This has come at the expense of other Asian geographies, as investors have believed (or perhaps hoped) that the Chinese economy is going to turn more positive in the months and years ahead. In the short term, we have witnessed a massive inflow of fresh capital into Chinese funds, while other areas have stalled:

This is not a "bad" thing for Chinese equities. In fact, it is quite good. It helps restore confidence and suggests that the earlier selling has tapped out. But my followers know I like to position myself as more of a contrarian investor. So buying into - or recommending - ideas after the market has shifted this dramatically is not something I have a habit of doing.

This is a fundamental point. Whether or not the Chinese economy is about to turn the page is a projection. But the equity market has priced in that it will because investors saw others rushing in and "fear of missing out" took over a bit, in my view. So what we are left with now is equity prices that are elevated from earlier this year, but China still faces many of the same headwinds. In this type of environment, I see a lot of merit to being cautious. That is central to why I believe a downgrade to "hold" is the right call.

Why Not Be A Bear? Equities Are Relatively Cheap

I definitely have concerns about the broader economic climate in China, and also the fact that I personally feel equities there have gotten a bit ahead of themselves. I stand by that call going forward. But readers will note I placed "hold" on FXI, not "sell". This isn't to play both sides of the fence, but rather to exemplify that there are legitimate reasons for being optimistic on Chinese equities. This push-pull reality results in a backdrop where I would find it a bit too risky to be too bearish on this thematic idea.

To justify this, consider that Chinese equities had been underperforming the world until very recently. This has left them trading at valuations that are not only relatively cheap to their peers but also with regard to their own trading history:

For this simple reason, I think the downside potential is a bit limited right now. Yes, Chinese stocks could give up some of their recent gains. That is a big reason why I am not a buyer here. But I also see a bottom to how low they can go because valuations are historically cheap. Until this changes, it will be hard for me to suggest being a straight-line seller.

Distribution Signals Weakness

I will now shift the conversation to FXI specifically. This is relevant because I have a "hold" rating on this fund in isolation, and there are numerous ways to play this space. FXI holds predominately large-cap companies in China, which theoretically should be lower risk than smaller businesses.

However, I have concerns with this thesis because of what the distribution is telling us. FXI saw a dramatic drop in its payout in the first half of the year compared to 2023, which should raise some eyebrows:

June 2023 Distribution June 2024 Distribution YOY Change
$.154/share $.085/share (45%)

Source: iShares

This signals the underlying firms within FXI's portfolio have either cash-flow problems or management is concerned about future growth/profitability. Neither of these scenarios inspires a lot of confidence in me.

Of course, these companies could be curtailing dividend payouts in order to invest for future growth. That would be a worthwhile endeavor and a reasonable trade-off. But I don't think that is the case here. When it happens in isolation or across a particular sector, I could buy that story. But even as a single-country ETF, FXI is still a diversified fund and this speaks to a broader message of weakness in the underlying Chinese economy rather than prudent fiscal discipline on the part of corporate management. This is another supporting factor for downgrading my rating right now.

This Fund Is Heavily Dependent On The Consumer

The next topic for discussion is the make-up of FXI's holdings. Specifically, I want to explain that the fund's heavy tilt toward the Consumer Discretionary sector is a source of concern for me. This is no small attribute for this fund, with this one sector alone representing over 1/3 of total assets:

For me, the forward outlook for retail and discretionary spend within China is very mixed. Yes, the government has announced some stimulus measures and brighter days could be ahead. The message from the central government only goes so far to actual spending. To predict how loose consumers will be with their wallets in the future, we need to consider consumer/household sentiment. On this metric alone, things look quite scary:

It is hard to sugarcoat this metric. To be fair, the contrarian bell could be ringing for some - and I wouldn't necessarily fault them. But we should recognize that consumer sentiment is a broad measure of how the Chinese feel about their current and future prospects, and it isn't going to rapidly change overnight. This is going to take time and, even if it does improve, the current level is so depressed that a moderate rise in this metric won't do much to improve my own confidence about Chinese equities going forward.

Stimulus Announcements Need To Have Teeth

My final point touches on the upcoming change in Chinese fiscal and monetary policy going forward. As the market has reacted positively to news that the current government is going to take steps to give the domestic economy a boost. Among the steps the government is considering are detailed below:

All good news for investors, right? In fairness, it may be. But I would interject with a tone of caution here for two reasons.

One, the market has already rallied on the news. The best time to buy would have been before this announcement. Buying in now is a bit late to the game in the sense that I would want to see actual results, not just policy actions. Can equities rally still? They could, but I see the recent pullback off the highs for funds like FXI as a sign the market's euphoria is cooling a bit.

Two, what the Chinese government wants to do and what it actually does can be two different things. And this isn't meant to be an insult or a slight, just an observation of how previous announcements have not always materialized.

For example, if we look at planned spending for 2024 (before the recent announcement) we see that local government spending has actually trailed anticipated spending for the year. In fact, this lag was pretty consistent and meaningful across the board:

What I take away from this is that I need to see action more than words when it comes to my investment strategy within China. When I hear plans for the future, I perk up, but I will not take plans to the bank. I will wait for some signs of actual change and/or results before committing my hard-earned dollars toward new policy goals in China. This is especially true for funds like FXI that are singularly centered on this country. For me, this is confirmation that a downgrade to "hold" is the right move.

Bottom Line

Chinese stocks got a big boost of late and that was certainly a welcome move for the bulls. While we could see brighter days ahead due to increased government spending, fewer regulations, and lower interest rates, I would caution against getting too carried away. The market has already rewarded equities with a big push higher, and that tells me further gains will be more difficult to come by. Investors will need to see improved profit figures and not just slogans from the government. That is going to take time.

Further, consumer sentiment is extremely negative right now, and that means spending is not likely to impress in the months ahead. For funds like FXI that rely heavily on the consumer discretionary sector, this poses a headwind. As a result, I believe a rating shift to a more neutral stance is appropriate, and I would suggest to my followers they approach any new positions very selectively at this time.

data.title: Arcosa: Still Not Cheap Enough
data.description:
The Thesis

Arcosa (NYSE: NYSE:ACA) continued its topline growth after a weak 2023, as it exited the first half of the year due to steady growth in the construction product segment and benefit from contribution from the recent acquisition of Ameron in the engineered structures segment.

I expect this growth to continue further in the rest of 2024 as demand remains healthy across most of the business with healthy order activity, which along with benefits from backlog and continued contribution from acquisition should help in sustaining topline growth in the quarters ahead. Margins should also remain good with pricing strength and benefits from operational improvement. Since my last article on ACA, the stock has been up in the low double-digit underperforming the market. Currently, the stock is trading at a premium to both its historical average and sector median.

While the company's long-term appears to be promising primarily due to the new Stavola acquisition, its forward growth rate with mid-single-digit EPS growth does not justify this premium valuation to me. Therefore, I would like to stay on the sidelines for now, as I expect this underperformance to continue further.

Last Quarter Performance

The company’s top line was up in the low single digits in 2023, followed by lower revenue in the Engineered segment due to the divestiture of its storage tank business. However, the first half has been good so far, with its revenue increasing by 13.7% to $664.7 million, as it exited the first half of 2024. This double-digit growth resulted from a strong 33% year-on-year increase in Engineered Structures segment revenue, which primarily benefited from higher volume in utility structure and contribution from a recent acquisition, Ameron. The Construction Product segment on the other hand was up mid-single digits due to the benefit of strong pricing in its aggregated business, more than offsetting the impact from volume decline. The Transportation Products segment was nearly flat with a 0.7% year-on-year increase in the second quarter of 2024.

Higher revenue across all the segments also benefitted the company’s margin during the quarter as its adjusted EBITDA jumped 240 bps to 17%. This was driven by strong margins in the construction segment, which saw an increase of nearly 360 bps, versus the prior year's same quarter due to benefits from strong pricing and operational improvements in certain parts of this business. EBITDA growth also benefited the company's bottom line growth as its adjusted EPS increased to $0.91 from $0.76 a year ago, beating the consensus estimates by $0.08 in the second quarter of 2024.

Outlook

As the company exited the first half of the year, its topline continued its steady growth after a slowdown in 2023, as the company benefited from significant contributions from recent acquisitions and favorable pricing across some parts of the business. I expect this growth to continue further in the latter half of the year, as the demand environment remains healthy, primarily in the utility structure and wind tower business of the Engineered structures segment, which along with a strong $1.3 billion backlog related to wind and utility structure should drive topline growth in the quarters ahead. In addition to this, the strong pricing momentum also continued across the company’s construction product segment, which along with expected volume improvement in the coming quarters, after the recent decline due to elevated rainfall in the region, should benefit the company’s revenue in the quarter ahead.

Talking about the Transportation product segment, positive momentum continued in liquid barges order booking as the company exited the first half of the year. In the last quarter itself, total barge orders were approximately $33 million, which largely includes tank barges. The barge-related backlog also remains elevated at nearly $252 million as of the end of the second quarter, with 70% expected to be converted in the remainder of 2024. In my view, this should further support the company’s consolidated revenue growth in 2024.

While the near-term prospects look favorable for the company, contributions from new upcoming acquisitions and benefits from project completions should drive topline growth beyond 2024. In 2023, the company announced to acquire Stavola, a leading provider of construction materials, for $1.2 billion. This is the largest acquisition in the company’s history that is expected to enhance the company’s portfolio significantly and expand its market presence across the largest MSA in the U.S., which should further strengthen the company's market position in the region. In addition to this, the company has also invested significantly in certain projects in the past two years to drive long-term organic growth, which is expected to be completed by year-end 2024.

As strategic acquisitions remain a key part of the company’s long-term growth, it continued to focus on potential M&As that strategically fit and help the company enhance its offerings and expand its geographic footprint. Currently, the company’s leverage ratio stands at 3.7 times, which is notably higher than its targeted ratio of 2 to 2.5 times, primarily due to the financing for the Stavola acquisition. However, the company remains focused on reducing debt, using proceeds from the sale of some non-core and loss-making businesses such as the steel components business and subscale asphalt business. In my view, this should result in improved leverage in the coming years, further supporting future acquisitions and benefiting the company’s topline growth in the long term.

Valuation

I last covered ACA earlier this year in February and gave it a hold rating due to its premium valuation as compared to its historical average and sector median. Since then, the stock has been underperforming and is currently up nearly 11% against a 17% increase in the S&P 500. Currently, the company's stock is trading at a forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 27.15, based on the FY24 EPS of $3.54, representing a mid-single-digit growth year on year. In comparison with its five-year average of 24.64, the stock looks slightly overvalued. In comparison with its sector median of 20.81x on the other hand, the stock is trading at approximately a 31% premium.

Company Forward P/E (non-GAAP) Forward Revenue growth Forward EBITDA growth
(ACA) Arcosa 27.15x 9.61% 18.71%
(STRL) Sterling Infrastructure 29.01x 9.60% 16.95%
(ROAD) Construction Partners 59.23x 16.92% 32.81%
(PRIM) Primoris Services Corporation 18.97x 13.84% 17.38%
(DY) Dycom Industries 25.63x 11.27% 21.95%

As we can see in the table above, while the company is expected to continue its growth across both the top line and margins, it does not provide the best value against its closest peers, which are also expected to grow at a similar or better forward growth rate with an even better multiple.

I expect the company's revenue to benefit from healthy demand, favorable pricing, and contribution from the recent acquisition, which along with a focus on operation improvement should benefit the margins in the longer term. In addition to this, focusing on reducing debt through the proceeds of the sale of non-core businesses and contribution from the new higher margin Stavola acquisition should help the bottom-line growth in the coming year, which could improve valuation in the future. However, considering the current multiple and single-digit EPS growth rate for FY24, I consider the stock valuation too expensive at the moment.

Conclusion

As we discussed earlier, the company's stock is trading above both its sector median and historical average. The near term looks favorable as demand remains good in certain parts of the business which along with support from recent acquisitions, the company should continue its top-line growth in 2024. Strong pricing on the other hand should drive margins in the quarters ahead. While the long-term growth prospects remain good, I would suggest a 'Hold' rating on this stock at the current levels due to its premium valuation.
